sanskrit_devanagari:
तपस्विभ्योऽधिको योगी ज्ञानिभ्योऽपि मतोऽधिकः ।
कर्मिभ्यश्चाधिको योगी तस्माद्योगी भवार्जुन ॥ ४६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
tapasvibhyo 'dhiko yogī jñānibhyo 'pi mato 'dhikaḥ |
karmibhyaś cādhiko yogī tasmād yogī bhavārjuna || 46 ||

anvaya.meaning: than the ascetics / those performing austerity
than the men of knowledge / scriptural learning

translations.verbatim_quote: A yogī is greater than the ascetic, greater than the empiricist and greater than the fruitive worker. Therefore, O Arjuna, in all circumstances, be a yogī.
A yogi is greater than the ascetic, greater than the empiricist and greater than the fruitive worker. Therefore, O Arjuna, in all circumstances, be a yogi.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1103 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: When we speak of yoga we refer to linking our consciousness with the Supreme Absolute Truth. Such a process is named differently by various practitioners in terms of the particular method adopted.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.46 A yogi is adhikah, higher; tapasvibhyah, than men of austerity; he is matah, considered; adhikah, higher than, superior to; api, even; jnanibhyah, men of knowledge. Jnana here means scriptural learning. (A yogi is superior) to even those who possess that (learning). The yogi is adhika
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-46-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the threefold superiority strictly: the yogi is greater than the ascetics (those who perform tapas), greater than the men of scriptural-learning (jñānī here = those who possess Vedic learning, NOT the realised non-dualist knower), and greater than those who perform ritual works for fruit (karmins). Therefore, Krishna instructs Arjuna: be a yogi. The yogi-of-disciplined-action sits structurally above three named alternatives.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.46 Whatever end of human endeavour is attained by mere austerity, by knowledge of different subjects (i.e., different from experience of the self) and by mere rituals like the horse-sacrifice etc., —- greater than all these is the end achieved through Yoga. Consequently the Yogin is supe
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-46-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja sharpens the comparison to ends-attained: whatever human end is reached through mere austerity, through knowledge of subjects-other-than-self, or through mere rituals like the horse-sacrifice, the end attained through Yoga is greater. Consequently the Yogin is superior to all three. The hierarchy is structural and ranked by the destination each path actually reaches.

positions.rendering: men of scriptural learning (Vedic learning), NOT the realised non-dualist
those with knowledge of subjects other than the self

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Critical disagreement. Shankara explicitly narrows jñānī here to *those who possess scriptural learning* — emphatically NOT the realised jñānin (the non-dualist knower of Brahman, who is identical to the supreme yogi). This protects the verse from a contradiction with Advaita's hierarchy elsewhere. Ramanuja narrows similarly: knowledge of subjects *other than* the experience of self. Prabhupada's 'empiricist' is the loosest rendering. Across traditions, the verse compares the yogi to specific limited categories (austerity-only, scripture-only, ritual-only) and ranks the integrated yogi above each.

literal_meaning: The yogi is greater than the ascetics; he is considered greater even than the men of knowledge; the yogi is greater than the ritualists. Therefore, Arjuna, be a yogi.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.46 is the chapter's penultimate verse and the structural hierarchy claim. Krishna names three categories — the ascetic (tapasvī, performer of austerities), the man of scriptural learning (jñānī, where 'jñānī' is read by Shankara as the scripture-possessor, not the realised non-dualist; by Ramanuja as the knower of non-self subjects), and the ritualist (karmī, the fruit-seeker performing prescribed rites) — and ranks the yogi above all three. Krishna then issues the direct imperative: tasmād yogī bhava arjuna — 'therefore, Arjuna, be a yogi.' The verse compresses the entire chapter into a hierarchy claim and a directive: the integrated yogi-of-disciplined-action surpasses the partial paths of mere austerity, mere learning, and mere ritual.
